title: execution-result-v1
description: Output contract, executor -> platform: report.json. DERIVED from internal/events.RunReport. Additional top-level properties are tolerated (additive evolution), under the same recursive redaction rule as execution-event-v1. Cross-field rules (finished_at >= started_at) and artifact path confinement live in the semantic validators.

format_version.description: Document format version. Absent, zero, and future versions are rejected. This is the DOCUMENT version, not the executor build version.

run_id.description: Run identifier; same rules as events.ValidateRunID.

version.description: The EXECUTOR BUILD version (internal/version, set via ldflags). This is NOT the document format version — that is format_version. The two are deliberately distinct fields and must never be merged.

mode.description: What the executor did. Note the hyphen in dry-run: execution-spec-v1.mode uses dry_run with an underscore. Input and output speak different mode vocabularies.

scope.title: report scope
scope.description: events.ReportScope: a record of what ran. Unlike the spec's scope it has no 'at least one true' rule — an account-inventory report legitimately carries all three false.

source.title: host reference
source.description: events.HostRef. A non-pointer Go struct, so `omitempty` never fires: the key is always present in the JSON, and both members are always present, possibly as "". Never carries a password, a key, or a port.

destination.title: host reference
destination.description: events.HostRef. A non-pointer Go struct, so `omitempty` never fires: the key is always present in the JSON, and both members are always present, possibly as "". Never carries a password, a key, or a port.

finished_at.description: Must not precede started_at; enforced by the semantic validators.

artifacts.description: Artifact name -> path, omitted when empty. Paths are slash-separated and relative to the run workspace. The platform resolves them against a directory it owns, so the semantic validators reject absolute paths, Windows drive letters, UNC prefixes, '..' segments (under either separator) and NUL bytes: an escaping path is a write-anywhere primitive.
